content:
8 min read
2 hours ago

--

A step-by-step guide to embedding Mozilla’s SpiderMonkey engine and building your own JavaScript runtime from scratch.
When developers write JavaScript, they usually think about the language itself :
console.log("Hello, World!");or
setTimeout(() => {
console.log("Hello after 1 second");
}, 1000);It looks simple, but something has to understand, compile, and execute this code.
Many developers use Node.js, Bun, Deno, or a web browser every day without thinking about what happens underneath. The truth is that these are JavaScript runtimes, not JavaScript engines.
Understanding the difference between the two is the first step toward building your own runtime.
What is a JavaScript Engine?
A JavaScript engine is software that understands the JavaScript language.
Its job is to:

• Parse JavaScript source code

• Compile it into executable instructions

• Execute the code

• Manage memory with garbage collection
For example, if you write:
const x = 5 + 10;representation, and executes it.
A JavaScript engine only understands the language.
It does not know how to:

• Read files

• Open network connections

• Create timers

• Listen for HTTP requests

• Access the operating system
Popular JavaScript engines include:
Engines : V8, SpiderMonkey, JavaScriptCore, and QuickJS.
Used By : Chrome, Node.js,Bun,Firefox,Safari and Embedded applications.
What is a JavaScript Runtime?
A JavaScript runtime is everything required to execute real JavaScript applications.
A runtime includes:

• A JavaScript engine

• Native APIs

• An event loop

• Timers

• Module loading

• File system APIs

• Networking APIs

• Operating system integration
Think of it like this: JavaScript Runtime then
+-----------------------------+
| JavaScript APIs |
| console.log() |
| setTimeout() |
| fetch() |
| fs.readFile() |
+-------------+---------------+
|
+-------------v---------------+
| Event Loop |
+-------------+---------------+
|
+-------------v---------------+
| JavaScript Engine |
| Parser |
| Compiler |
| Garbage Collector |
+-------------+---------------+
|
+-------------v---------------+
| Operating System |
+-----------------------------The runtime is responsible for connecting JavaScript to the operating system.
Engine vs Runtime
A useful analogy is to compare them to a car.
The engine provides the power needed to move the vehicle.
The runtime is the complete car.
A car needs much more than an engine:

• Steering

• Brakes

• Wheels

• Dashboard

• Fuel system

• Transmission
Without those components, an engine alone cannot transport anyone.
The same is true for JavaScript.
A JavaScript engine can execute code, but a runtime makes it practical for building real applications.
Why Browsers Feel Different from Node.js
Consider this example:
document.querySelector("h1");This works in a browser because the browser runtime provides the DOM API.
Now consider:
const fs = require("fs");This works in Node.js because Node.js provides the File System API.
Neither API exists inside the JavaScript language itself.
The engine simply executes JavaScript. The runtime decides which APIs are available.
Where Does SpiderMonkey Fit?
SpiderMonkey is Mozilla’s JavaScript engine.
It is responsible for:

• Parsing JavaScript

• Compiling JavaScript

• Executing JavaScript

• Managing memory
However, SpiderMonkey does not provide APIs like:

• console.log()

• setTimeout()

• setInterval()

• fetch()

• File system access
Those APIs must be implemented by the runtime embedding SpiderMonkey.

What is Forge?
Forge is a JavaScript runtime built on top of SpiderMonkey.
SpiderMonkey provides the JavaScript engine.
Forge adds everything around it:

• A command-line executable

• JavaScript file loading

• Native APIs

• Timers

• An event loop

• Operating system integration
The goal of this guide is to build those pieces step by step until we have a working JavaScript runtime capable of executing real JavaScript applications.
Before we can build our own JavaScript runtime, we first need the JavaScript engine itself.
Forge uses SpiderMonkey, Mozilla’s JavaScript engine, which powers Firefox. Instead of writing a JavaScript engine from scratch, we embed SpiderMonkey into our own executable and build runtime features around it.
In this , we’ll:

• Download the SpiderMonkey source code.

• Install the required build tools.

• Configure a SpiderMonkey-only build.

• Compile the JavaScript shell.

• Verify that the engine works correctly.
By the end of this , we’ll have a working SpiderMonkey build that we can embed into Forge.
Here’s a clean summary of the process we followed to embed SpiderMonkey on Linux.

• For Debian-based Linux (such as Ubuntu): sudo apt update && sudo apt install curl python3 python3-venv git make
Ubuntu 24.04 LTS (Recommended)
This is my recommendation.

• Supported until 2029

• New compiler versions

• Good CMake support

• Great for SpiderMonkey development

• Good for GitHub Actions (Ubuntu 24.04 runners)
Cloning the Source Code
The SpiderMonkey source lives inside Mozilla’s gecko-dev repository.
Clone it using Git:
git clone https://github.com/mozilla/gecko-dev.git
cd gecko-devThis downloads the complete Firefox source tree, including SpiderMonkey.
The repository is large because SpiderMonkey shares code with the rest of the Firefox project.
Bootstrapping the Build Environment
Before compiling, Mozilla provides a bootstrap script that installs the required dependencies.
Run:
./mach bootstrapDuring the setup you’ll be asked what you want to build.
Choose:
SpiderMonkey JavaScript engineThe bootstrap process installs tools such as:

• Python

• Rust

• Cargo

• Clang

• Build utilities

• Required development libraries
Without these tools, SpiderMonkey cannot be compiled.
Creating a MOZCONFIG
Mozilla’s build system is controlled by a configuration file called a MOZCONFIG.
Create one:
mkdir -p ~/mozconfigs
nano ~/mozconfigs/forgeAdd the following configuration:
ac_add_options --enable-project=jsac_add_options --enable-optimize
ac_add_options --disable-debug
mk_add_options MOZ_OBJDIR=@TOPSRCDIR@/obj-forge-@CONFIG_GUESS@
Let’s understand what each option does.

Building Only SpiderMonkey
ac_add_options --enable-project=jsFirefox contains many projects:
Firefox
├── Browser UI
├── CSS Engine
├── DOM
├── Media
├── Networking
└── SpiderMonkeyThis option tells Mozilla:
Build only the JavaScript engine.
Instead of compiling the entire browser, the build system skips everything except SpiderMonkey.
This significantly reduces build time.
Optimized Builds :
ac_add_options --enable-optimizeThis enables compiler optimizations.
Optimized builds execute faster and more closely match production builds
Disabling Debug Mode:
ac_add_options --disable-debugDebug builds include additional assertions and debugging checks.
For a runtime intended for normal use, a release build is usually preferable.
Choosing an Object Directory:
mk_add_options MOZ_OBJDIR=@TOPSRCDIR@/obj-forge-@CONFIG_GUESS@Mozilla separates source files from compiled files.
Instead of placing object files inside the source tree, everything is written into an object directory.
For example:
gecko-dev/
│
├── js/
├── browser/
├── toolkit/
└── obj-forge-x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/Keeping build artifacts separate makes it possible to have multiple independent build configurations without modifying the source tree.
Activating the Configuration
Tell Mozilla to use the configuration file:
export MOZCONFIG=$HOME/mozconfigs/forgeYou can verify it:
echo $MOZCONFIGExpected output:
/home/username/mozconfigs/forgeBuilding SpiderMonkey
Now compile the project:
./mach buildThe build system will:

• Read your MOZCONFIG.

• Generate the build backend.

• Compile SpiderMonkey.

• Link the JavaScript shell.
Depending on your hardware, this can take anywhere from several minutes to much longer.
Even on a 16-vCPU AWS Ubuntu instance, a full build takes time and keeps every core busy.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Understanding the Build Output
After a successful build, Mozilla creates an object directory.
Example:
obj-forge-x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/Inside it you’ll find:
obj-forge-x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/
│
├── dist/
│ └── bin/
│ ├── js
│ └── libmozjs-142a1.so
│
├── js/
│
└── ...The most important file is:
obj-forge-x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/dist/bin/jsThis is Mozilla’s standalone JavaScript shell.
Verifying the Build
Run:
./obj-forge-x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/dist/bin/js --versionExample output:
JavaScript-C142.0a1Now start the shell:
./obj-forge-x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/dist/bin/jsYou’ll see the SpiderMonkey prompt:
js>Try a simple program:
print("Hello from SpiderMonkey");Output:
Hello from SpiderMonkeyExit the shell:
quit();if this works, you’ve successfully built SpiderMonkey.
Why Build the JS Shell First?
Before embedding SpiderMonkey into our own runtime, we first verify that the engine itself works correctly.
The js executable serves as a reference implementation provided by Mozilla. It confirms that:

• SpiderMonkey compiled successfully.

• The JavaScript engine can execute code.

• The build environment is configured correctly.
Once this shell works, we can begin creating our own executable that embeds the same engine.
now , we successfully built SpiderMonkey and verified it using Mozilla’s JavaScript shell.
However, the js shell is Mozilla's executable—not ours.
The goal of this is to create our own executable, named Forge, and embed SpiderMonkey into it. At first, Forge won’t execute any JavaScript. We’ll simply create a program that links against SpiderMonkey and initializes the engine successfully.
By the end of this , we’ll have our own executable running alongside Mozilla’s js shell.
What Does “Embedding” Mean?
Embedding means including one piece of software inside another application.
Think about SQLite.
SQLite isn’t a separate server running on your computer. Instead, applications link against the SQLite library and use it directly.
SpiderMonkey works the same way.
Before+----------------+
| SpiderMonkey |
| JS Shell |
+----------------+
After
+----------------+
| Forge |
| |
| SpiderMonkey |
+----------------+
SpiderMonkey becomes a library inside our executable.

Creating the Forge Directory
Inside the Gecko source tree, create a new directory:
gecko-dev/
└── js/
└── src/
└── forge/This directory will contain the source code for our runtime.
Create two files:
forge/
├── forge.cpp
└── moz.buildTeaching the Build System About Forge
Creating a directory isn’t enough.
Mozilla’s build system doesn’t automatically scan every folder looking for source code. Instead, every directory must be explicitly registered.
Open:
js/src/moz.buildLocate:
if not CONFIG["JS_DISABLE_SHELL"]:Add:
DIRS += [
"rust",
"shell",
"forge",
]Now, every time ./mach build runs, the build system will also visit:
js/src/forge/and process its moz.build file.
Creating forge/moz.build
Create js/src/forge/moz.build
We initially used:
Program("forge")but that caused crashes before main().
The working version is:
Every buildable directory contains a moz.build file.
Create:
GeckoProgram("forge", linkage=None)USE_LIBS += [
"static:js",
]
include("../js-config.mozbuild")
include("../js-cxxflags.mozbuild")
include("../js-standalone.mozbuild")
UNIFIED_SOURCES += [
"forge.cpp",
]
DEFINES["EXPORT_JS_API"] = True
LOCAL_INCLUDES += [
"!..",
"..",
]
GeckoProgram("forge")
→ Create an executable named "forge"USE_LIBS += "static:js"
→ Use the SpiderMonkey library
include(...)
→ Use Mozilla's standard build settings
UNIFIED_SOURCES += "forge.cpp"
→ Compile forge.cpp
DEFINES["EXPORT_JS_API"] = True
→ Enable SpiderMonkey's public API
LOCAL_INCLUDES
→ Tell the compiler where to find header files
Initially, it describes our new executable and tells the build system where to find its source file. As Forge grows, this file will also list additional source files, libraries, compiler flags, and include paths.
The important idea is that moz.build is the build recipe for this directory.
Our First Forge Program
Let’s begin wi
